texto: So I kept reading, just to stay alive. In fact, I'd read two or three books at the same time, so I wouldn't finish one without being in the middle of another -- anything to stop me from falling into the big, gaping void. You see, books fill the empty spaces. If I'm waiting for a bus, or am eating alone, I can always rely on a book to keep me company. Sometimes I think I like them even more than people. People will let you down in life. They'll disappoint you and hurt you and betray you. But not books. They're better than life.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Marc Acito: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de passagem do tempo e prioridades. O trecho “So I kept reading, just to stay alive. In fact, I'd read two or three books at the same time, so …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, books, literature pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de literature e people.

In fact, I'd read two or three books at the same time, so I wouldn't finish one without being in the middle of another -- anything to stop me from falling into the big, gaping void. You see, books fill the empty spaces. If I'm waiting for a bus, or am eating alone, I can always rely on a book to keep me company. Sometimes I think I like them even more than people. People will let you down in life. They'll disappoint you and hurt you and betray you. But not books. They're better than life.

texto: I shouldn't be surprised. Catholicism is the ultimate loophole religion (sin, confess, repeat), so it makes sense that a priest would know better than anyone how to work the angles. Still, when you go to confession and say, "Forgive me, Father, for I have sinned," you don't expect him to say, "So, who hasn't?
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Marc Acito que provoca limites e escolhas conscientes. Ao questionar “I shouldn't be surprised. Catholicism is the ultimate loophole religion (sin, confess, repeat), s…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre familia, humor, religion, com foco em trabalho e religion.
